glossary.def: The principle that two portfolios paying identical amounts in every future state must cost the same today; otherwise a riskless profit could be locked in by buying the cheap one and selling the dear one. It is the only economic assumption the binomial model needs.

A position in the underlying stock and a risk-free bond, rebalanced so it reproduces an option's payoff in every state of the world. By the law of one price the option must cost exactly what the replicating portfolio costs — pricing is replication, not prediction.

The synthetic probability p = (e^{rΔt} − d)/(u − d) under which the discounted price of every traded asset is a martingale and the stock drifts at the risk-free rate. It is set entirely by the up/down factors and the rate; the stock's real-world expected return never enters.

Pricing a derivative as the discounted expectation of its payoff under the risk-neutral measure ℚ, rather than under real-world probabilities. The stock's drift μ cancels out, so two traders who disagree about direction still agree on the option price.

The number of shares Δ = (V_u − V_d)/(S₀(u − d)) held in the replicating portfolio to offset an option's exposure over one step — the spread of option values divided by the spread of stock values. It is the discrete ancestor of the Black–Scholes delta.

A recombining binomial lattice with u = e^{σ√Δt}, d = 1/u, priced by backward induction. The choice ud = 1 makes 'up then down' land on the same node as 'down then up', so N steps give only N+1 terminal nodes, and the price converges to Black–Scholes as N grows.

The algorithm that prices a tree from the leaves to the root: set terminal payoffs, then repeatedly replace each node by the discounted risk-neutral average of its two children. For American options each node also takes the max of that continuation value and immediate exercise.

The amount by which an American option exceeds its otherwise-identical European twin, V_American − V_European ≥ 0 — the value of the right to exercise before expiry. It is zero for a call on a non-dividend stock and strictly positive for a sufficiently in-the-money put at a positive interest rate.

The frontier in the (time, stock-price) plane separating the nodes where exercising an American option immediately beats holding it from the nodes where holding is optimal. For a put it is the curve of highest stock prices at which early exercise still pays; high rates push it upward.

refs.cite: Cox, J. C., Ross, S. A. & Rubinstein, M. (1979). Option Pricing: A Simplified Approach. Journal of Financial Economics 7(3).
Merton, R. C. (1973). Theory of Rational Option Pricing. Bell Journal of Economics and Management Science 4(1).
Hull, J. C. (2021). Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (11th ed.). Pearson.
Shreve, S. E. (2004). Stochastic Calculus for Finance I: The Binomial Asset Pricing Model. Springer.
